title: Policy Rules and Targets: Framing the Central Banker's Problem

source.name: Federal Reserve Bank of New York

summary: Stephen Cecchetti, Director of Research at the New York Fed, published an article in the June 1998 Economic Policy Review arguing that central banks adopting fixed inflation targets risk creating significant variability in output growth. Using data from 1984-95, Cecchetti demonstrates that the output-inflation variability trade-off is extremely steep—a modest decrease in inflation variability causes output to deviate substantially from its optimal path.
key_points: Central banks with rigid inflation targets may face sharp increases in GDP growth volatility as an unintended consequence
The output-inflation variability trade-off is extremely steep, meaning small reductions in inflation variability cause large output deviations
Rigid inflation targeting implicitly increases the weight placed on inflation variability relative to output variability in policy objectives
The Reserve Bank of Australia targets average inflation of 2-3 percent over the business cycle, allowing flexibility across cycles
New Zealand and UK central banks announce target ranges rather than point targets to retain output stabilization flexibility
Cecchetti concludes policymakers must ask whether they 'really care so little about output and other real quantities' when implementing price-only policies
why_it_matters: This 1998 academic analysis provides theoretical grounding for why flexible inflation targeting frameworks—allowing ranges rather than rigid point targets—became the preferred approach for major central banks. The research quantifies the trade-off costs of prioritizing price stability exclusively, informing subsequent monetary policy framework design. However, as a nearly 28-year-old document predating modern central bank frameworks (such as the Fed's 2012 formal inflation target), it is primarily of historical and academic interest.
macro_context: The article provides theoretical framework for understanding monetary policy trade-offs between inflation control and output stability. Cecchetti's analysis was contemporaneous with early adoption of formal inflation targeting by central banks globally, offering critique and guidance for framework design. No specific forward-looking market implications or current policy relevance can be extracted given the source's age.
important_numbers: 2-3 percent (Reserve Bank of Australia target range over business cycle)

issues_found: Source is a press release summarizing a research article rather than the full research paper itself—moderate reduction in archival depth
Published June 22, 1998—nearly 28 years before TODAY. While the source has historical academic value, it lacks contemporary market relevance and cannot inform current analysis

SOURCE METADATA lists TARGET/TOPIC as 'market structure' but content is entirely about monetary policy frameworks and inflation targeting theory; generated record correctly retagged as 'macro catalysts'
